attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
# Theme Factory Skill - Content Summary

**Overview**: The Theme Factory is a styling toolkit designed to apply professional, cohesive visual themes to various artifacts including presentations, documents, and web pages.

**Core Functionality**: The skill offers 10 pre-designed themes featuring carefully curated color palettes and complementary font pairings, with the option to generate custom themes when needed.

**Available Themes**: Ocean Depths, Sunset Boulevard, Forest Canopy, Modern Minimalist, Golden Hour, Arctic Frost, Desert Rose, Tech Innovation, Botanical Garden, and Midnight Galaxy.

**Primary Workflow**:
1. Display the theme showcase for visual reference
2. Request user preference
3. Obtain explicit confirmation of selection
4. Apply chosen colors and fonts throughout the artifact

**Key Feature**: Users can create custom themes by providing descriptions or specifications. The system generates new theme options following the established format, presents them for approval, then applies them consistently across the work.

**Quality Standards**: All themes ensure proper contrast and readability while maintaining distinct visual identities appropriate for different contexts and audiences.

content.content:
# Frontend Design Skill Overview

This skill enables creation of distinctive, production-grade web interfaces that prioritize authentic design over generic aesthetics.

## Core Purpose

The skill guides developers in building visually striking frontend components, pages, and applications by combining strategic design thinking with meticulous implementation. It emphasizes avoiding "AI slop"—predictable, cookie-cutter designs lacking intentionality.

## Key Design Principles

**Before coding**, establish a clear aesthetic direction by considering:
- The interface's purpose and target audience
- A distinct tonal approach (ranging from brutalist to maximalist, retro-futuristic to organic)
- Technical constraints and accessibility needs
- What makes the design genuinely memorable

**Implementation priorities** include:
- Distinctive typography choices that elevate rather than default to common fonts
- Cohesive color systems using CSS variables with intentional accent colors
- Strategic animation focusing on high-impact moments rather than scattered effects
- Unexpected spatial compositions employing asymmetry, overlap, and negative space
- Atmospheric details like gradients, textures, and contextual effects

## What to Avoid

The guidelines explicitly warn against overused choices including "generic font families" like Inter and Roboto, "cliched color schemes (particularly purple gradients)," and "predictable layouts" that lack context-specific character.

## Implementation Philosophy

Success requires matching code complexity to the aesthetic vision—maximalist designs warrant elaborate implementations, while refined minimalism demands restraint and precision. The emphasis is on executing a coherent vision rather than defaulting to industry conventions.

content.content:
# Slack GIF Creator Toolkit Overview

This documentation describes a Python toolkit for creating Slack-optimized animated GIFs. Here are the key takeaways:

## Technical Specifications
The tool requires specific dimensions: "Emoji GIFs: 128x128 (recommended)" and "Message GIFs: 480x480," with frame rates between 10-30 FPS and 48-128 colors for optimal results.

## Core Components
The toolkit includes several utilities:
- **GIFBuilder**: Assembles and optimizes frames for Slack compatibility
- **Validators**: Confirms GIFs meet Slack requirements
- **Easing Functions**: Enables smooth motion transitions
- **Frame Helpers**: Provides convenience functions for common drawing tasks

## Animation Approaches
The documentation covers multiple animation concepts including shake, pulse, bounce, rotation, fade effects, sliding, zooming, and particle bursts—each with implementation guidance using Python's PIL library.

## Design Philosophy
Rather than providing rigid templates, the toolkit emphasizes creative flexibility using PIL primitives (circles, polygons, lines, rectangles) to draw graphics from scratch or work with user-uploaded images based on their specific requests.

## File Optimization
When size reduction is needed, strategies include reducing frame counts, limiting colors to 48, using smaller dimensions, removing duplicate frames, and enabling emoji-specific optimization modes.

content.content:
# PDF Processing Guide - Complete Content

## Overview
This is a comprehensive guide for PDF operations using Python libraries and command-line tools. The document covers reading, extracting, merging, splitting, and creating PDFs.

## Key Python Libraries Covered

**pypdf** handles basic operations like merging multiple documents, splitting individual pages, extracting metadata, and rotating pages.

**pdfplumber** specializes in "text and table extraction" with layout preservation capabilities and advanced DataFrame conversion options.

**reportlab** enables PDF creation from scratch, supporting multiple pages, styled text, and spacing controls. The guide specifically warns: "Never use Unicode subscript/superscript characters" as they render as black boxes; use XML markup tags instead.

## Command-Line Tools

The guide references three tools:
- **pdftotext**: Text extraction with layout preservation
- **qpdf**: Page manipulation and merging
- **pdftk**: Legacy tool for merging and rotating pages

## Specialized Tasks

Instructions are provided for OCR on scanned documents (requiring pytesseract), watermarking, image extraction, and password protection.

## Important Note

The license is proprietary, with complete terms in LICENSE.txt. The guide directs users needing form-filling assistance to consult FORMS.md and recommends REFERENCE.md for advanced features and JavaScript library information.

content.content:
# Understanding the Algorithmic Art Framework

This document outlines a comprehensive system for creating generative art using p5.js, structured around three core phases:

## The Process

**Phase 1: Algorithmic Philosophy**
Create a 4-6 paragraph manifesto describing the computational aesthetic movement. The philosophy should articulate how ideas manifest through noise functions, particle behaviors, field dynamics, and emergent complexity. Critically, the documentation emphasizes repeatedly that final implementations must "appear as though it took countless hours to develop, was refined with care, and comes from someone at the absolute top of their field."

**Phase 2: Conceptual Deduction**
Extract the subtle thematic seed from user requests—refined references woven invisibly into parameters and behaviors rather than literal representations.

**Phase 3: p5.js Implementation**
Express the philosophy through self-contained HTML artifacts using seeded randomness, parametric controls, and real-time generation.

## Key Technical Requirements

- **Seeded Randomness**: Always use `randomSeed()` and `noiseSeed()` for reproducibility
- **Parameter Design**: Focus on system properties (quantities, scales, probabilities, thresholds) rather than preset "patterns"
- **Single Artifact Structure**: All code inline in one HTML file—no external dependencies except p5.js CDN
- **Template Foundation**: Start from `templates/viewer.html` without altering fixed sections (header, sidebar structure, Anthropic branding, seed controls, action buttons)

## Critical Implementation Principles

The system prioritizes **algorithmic beauty over static composition**—each execution produces unique outputs from identical code. Balance complexity without noise, maintain performance optimization, and ensure visual hierarchy even within randomness.

The framework treats the template as mandatory foundation rather than inspiration, preserving consistent UX while allowing complete algorithmic freedom in implementation.

content.content:
# Canvas Design Skill Overview

This skill guides creation of visual art through a two-step process: first establishing a **design philosophy** (an aesthetic manifesto), then **expressing it visually** on canvas as .pdf or .png files.

## Core Approach

The methodology emphasizes:

- **Visual-first communication**: "Ideas communicate through space, form, color, composition—not paragraphs"
- **Minimal text integration**: Text functions as visual accent, never explanation
- **Expert craftsmanship appearance**: Work should demonstrate meticulous labor and master-level execution
- **Conceptual subtlety**: Embedded references should feel intuitive to informed viewers while standing as sophisticated abstract composition

## Key Process Steps

1. **Philosophy Creation** (4-6 paragraphs): Establish aesthetic direction covering form, space, color, rhythm, and composition without redundancy
2. **Conceptual Threading**: Identify subtle thematic DNA woven into design
3. **Canvas Execution**: Translate philosophy into single or multi-page museum-quality work using systematic visual language and intentional color palettes

## Execution Standards

- Flawless composition with proper margins and breathing room
- Typography treated as design element, selected from curated fonts
- Patterns and repeated elements building meaning through sustained viewing
- Clinical precision combined with human craftsmanship appearance
- Nothing overlapping; all elements contained with perfect spacing

The final deliverable should appear as though created by someone "at the absolute top of their field" with "countless hours" of refinement.

content.content:
# PPTX Skill Overview

This documentation describes a presentation creation and editing toolkit. Here are the key takeaways:

## Core Capabilities

The skill handles any `.pptx` file interaction—creating decks, reading content, editing slides, and combining files. Use it whenever presentations or "slides" are mentioned.

## Reading Presentations

Extract text using `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx`, generate thumbnails, or unpack raw XML for detailed analysis.

## Creation Approaches

**From template:** Unpack existing slides, modify content, then repack.

**From scratch:** Use PptxGenJS when no reference material exists.

## Design Principles

Avoid plain text slides. Each slide needs visual elements—images, charts, or icons. Key recommendations include:

- Select topic-specific color palettes with one dominant color (60-70% weight)
- Employ contrasting dark/light backgrounds strategically
- Repeat one distinctive visual motif throughout
- Vary layouts to prevent monotony
- Maintain consistent 0.3-0.5" spacing and 0.5" margins
- Use 36-44pt titles and 14-16pt body text

## Quality Assurance Workflow

Assume initial renders contain issues. Check for missing content, placeholder text, overlapping elements, text overflow, and contrast problems. Use subagents for visual inspection by converting slides to JPEG images, then systematically fix and re-verify issues before completion.

content.content:
# DOCX Skill Documentation Summary

## Core Purpose
This skill handles creation, reading, editing, and manipulation of Word documents (.docx files), including professional formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, and letterheads.

## Key Capabilities

**Creating Documents:**
- Uses `docx-js` JavaScript library via `npm install -g docx`
- Must explicitly set page size (defaults to A4, not US Letter)
- Supports tables, images, hyperlinks, footnotes, headers/footers, and multi-column layouts

**Editing Existing Documents:**
Three-step process: unpack XML → edit → repack with validation

**Reading Content:**
- Extract text via `pandoc`
- Access raw XML through unpacking

## Critical Technical Rules

- **Page dimensions:** US Letter = 12,240 × 15,840 DXA (1,440 DXA = 1 inch)
- **Tables:** Require both `columnWidths` array AND individual cell widths in DXA units; widths must sum correctly
- **Never use:** Unicode bullets manually, newline characters, percentage-based table widths, or `ShadingType.SOLID`
- **Images require:** Type parameter (png/jpg/etc) and alternative text properties
- **Smart quotes:** Use XML entities (`“`, `’`, etc.) for professional typography

## XML Editing Guidelines

- Use "Claude" as default author for tracked changes
- Apply tracked changes via `<w:ins>` and `<w:del>` elements with metadata
- When deleting entire paragraphs, mark paragraph marks as deleted to prevent orphaned content
- Comment markers must be paragraph-level siblings, never nested inside runs

## Validation & Conversion

Auto-repair fixes invalid `durableId` values and missing `xml:space="preserve"` attributes during packing.

content.content:
# Web Artifacts Builder - Complete File Content

## Overview
The Web Artifacts Builder is a toolkit for creating sophisticated Claude.ai HTML artifacts using modern frontend technologies including React, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui components.

## Key Features

**Technology Stack**: "React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Parcel (bundling) + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui"

The system uses a three-phase workflow:
1. Initialize a new React project via initialization script
2. Develop the artifact by modifying generated files
3. Bundle everything into a single, self-contained HTML file

## Development Process

**Initial Setup**: Running the initialization script generates a fully configured project with React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3.4.1, pre-installed shadcn/ui components, and Parcel bundling configuration.

**Bundling**: The bundling script processes your project into `bundle.html`, "a self-contained artifact with all JavaScript, CSS, and dependencies inlined."

## Design Principles

The documentation emphasizes avoiding common aesthetic pitfalls: excessive centered layouts, purple gradients, uniform rounded corners, and Inter font usage that contribute to what practitioners call "AI slop."

## Additional Resources

- shadcn/ui component documentation available at ui.shadcn.com/docs/components
- License terms detailed in LICENSE.txt
- Testing is optional and recommended only after presenting the artifact if issues arise

content.content:
# Web Application Testing Toolkit

This resource provides guidance for testing local web applications using Playwright with Python.

## Key Components

**Available Tools:**
- `scripts/with_server.py` - Manages server lifecycle for single or multiple servers
- Example scripts in `examples/` directory demonstrating common patterns

## Recommended Workflow

The documentation suggests a decision tree approach:

1. For static HTML, read the file directly to find selectors, then write Playwright scripts
2. For dynamic applications, determine if the server is running
3. If not running, use the helper script to start it
4. If running, follow a "reconnaissance-then-action" pattern: navigate with waits, capture screenshots, identify selectors, then execute actions

## Critical Best Practices

The guidance emphasizes: "Wait for `page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')` before inspection" on dynamic applications to ensure JavaScript execution completes before attempting DOM inspection.

## Approach for Scripts

Rather than reading source code directly, users should "run: python scripts/with_server.py --help" first to understand usage as a black-box tool, avoiding unnecessary context window consumption.

The toolkit supports verification of frontend functionality, UI debugging, screenshot capture, and browser logging across local web applications.

content.content:
# Internal Communications Skill Overview

This skill helps you craft internal communications using your company's preferred formats. Here's what it covers:

**Communication types supported:**
- 3P updates (Progress, Plans, Problems)
- Company newsletters
- FAQ responses
- Status reports
- Leadership updates
- Project updates
- Incident reports

**How it works:**
The skill uses a straightforward process—you identify your communication type, then the system loads the relevant guideline file from the examples directory. Each guideline provides specific formatting, tone, and content requirements tailored to that communication style.

**Available guidelines:**
The skill references four main template files: one for 3P-style team updates, one for organization-wide newsletters, one for FAQ answers, and one for general communications that don't fit other categories.

If your communication needs don't align with existing guidelines, you can request clarification or additional context about your preferred format.

content.content:
# Anthropic Brand Guidelines - Complete File Content

## Document Overview

This file contains Anthropic's official brand styling specifications for applying consistent visual identity across artifacts.

## Brand Color Palette

The guidelines specify six core colors:

- **Primary Dark**: `#141413` for text and dark backgrounds
- **Primary Light**: `#faf9f5` for light backgrounds
- **Mid Gray**: `#b0aea5` for secondary elements
- **Light Gray**: `#e8e6dc` for subtle backgrounds
- **Orange Accent**: `#d97757`
- **Blue Accent**: `#6a9bcc`
- **Green Accent**: `#788c5d`

## Typography Standards

Headings use Poppins font (Arial backup), while body content uses Lora font (Georgia backup). The system automatically applies appropriate fallbacks when custom fonts aren't available on a given system.

## Key Implementation Features

The skill intelligently applies styling through:

- Font selection based on text size (24pt+ receives heading treatment)
- Automatic system font fallback mechanisms
- Accent color cycling for non-text elements
- RGB color precision for brand consistency
- Readability preservation across platforms

## Technical Foundation

The system leverages python-pptx's RGBColor class for color application and requires no font installation, operating seamlessly with pre-installed system fonts.

content.content:
# Summary of XLSX Skill Requirements

This document outlines comprehensive guidelines for Excel file creation, editing, and analysis.

## Core Purpose
The xlsx skill handles spreadsheet tasks including opening, reading, editing, creating new files, and converting tabular formats. It triggers when spreadsheets are the primary deliverable—not for Word documents, HTML reports, or database pipelines.

## Key Formatting Standards

**Professional Presentation**: Use consistent, professional fonts (Arial, Times New Roman) throughout all deliverables.

**Formula Integrity**: "Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)"

**Financial Color Coding** (unless otherwise specified):
- Blue text: Hardcoded inputs and user-changeable values
- Black text: Formulas and calculations
- Green text: Cross-sheet links within the workbook
- Red text: External file references
- Yellow background: Critical assumptions requiring updates

**Number Formatting Rules**:
- Years as text strings ("2024")
- Currency with units in headers ("Revenue ($mm)")
- Zeros displayed as dashes
- Percentages at 0.0% format
- Negative numbers in parentheses

## Critical Implementation Rules

**Use Formulas, Not Hardcoded Values**: Excel formulas must perform all calculations rather than computing values in Python and embedding results.

**Documentation Requirements**: Hardcoded values require source citations including document, date, specific reference, and URLs where applicable.

**Formula Recalculation**: After creating or modifying files with formulas, run `scripts/recalc.py` to calculate values and detect errors.

**Library Selection**: Use pandas for data analysis; openpyxl for formulas and formatting.

content.content:
# MCP Server Development Guide - Complete Content

This comprehensive guide provides a structured four-phase approach for building high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers that enable language models to interact with external services.

## Core Framework

**Four Development Phases:**

1. **Deep Research and Planning** - Understanding MCP design patterns, protocol specifications, and framework documentation
2. **Implementation** - Setting up project structure, core infrastructure, and tool development
3. **Review and Testing** - Code quality verification and functional testing
4. **Evaluation Creation** - Building comprehensive test suites for LLM effectiveness

## Key Design Principles

**API Coverage Strategy:**
The guide recommends balancing "comprehensive API endpoint coverage with specialized workflow tools." Workflow tools offer convenience for specific tasks, while comprehensive coverage provides agent flexibility.

**Tool Development Standards:**
Tools require clear naming conventions using consistent prefixes (like `github_create_issue`), actionable error messages with specific next steps, and structured output schemas using `structuredContent` for client compatibility.

**Technical Stack Recommendation:**
TypeScript is recommended as the primary language due to "high-quality SDK support and good compatibility in many execution environments," alongside Streamable HTTP for remote servers and stdio for local implementations.

## Implementation Resources

The guide provides language-specific implementation paths:
- **TypeScript**: Project structure patterns, Zod schemas, server registration examples
- **Python**: FastMCP patterns, Pydantic models, tool registration decorators

Testing uses the MCP Inspector tool (`npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector`), and evaluation requires creating 10 independent, complex, read-only test questions with verifiable answers in XML format.

content.content:
# Doc Co-Authoring Workflow Overview

This workflow guides users through collaborative document creation in three structured stages:

## Key Stages

**Stage 1: Context Gathering**
The process begins by closing knowledge gaps. As described, "User provides all relevant context while Claude asks clarifying questions." Users can share information through conversation, linked documents, or team channels when integrations are available.

**Stage 2: Refinement & Structure**
Documents are built section-by-section using a repeatable cycle: clarifying questions → brainstorming options → curation → drafting → iterative refinement. The workflow suggests starting with sections containing the most unknowns.

**Stage 3: Reader Testing**
The completed document is validated by testing it with fresh Claude instances (or manually in new conversations) to catch blind spots authors might miss.

## When to Trigger

Offer this workflow when users mention documentation tasks like writing specs, proposals, decision docs, or similar structured content. The approach emphasizes quality over speed and gives users agency to adjust the process.

## Notable Features

- Uses artifacts or files to scaffold document structure with placeholders
- Employs `str_replace` for surgical edits rather than full reprints
- Recommends starting complex sections first
- Includes a final user review step before completion
- Suggests linking the conversation as documentation of the development process

The workflow prioritizes ensuring documents actually work for readers who lack the authors' context.

content.content:
# Claude API Skill Overview

I'm Claude, an AI assistant built on Anthropic's Claude Agent SDK. This skill guides you through building LLM-powered applications using Claude.

## What I Do

I help you:
- **Build and debug Claude API applications** across Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, and cURL
- **Optimize prompt caching** to reduce costs and latency
- **Migrate code** between Claude model versions
- **Implement Claude features** like thinking, tool use, structured outputs, batches, and file handling
- **Design agents** using Claude API or Managed Agents (Anthropic's server-managed agent surface)

## Quick Start: Choosing Your Path

I detect your project language automatically. Then I guide you based on your needs:

| Need | Approach |
|------|----------|
| Single classification, Q&A, or summary | Claude API (one call) |
| Multi-step workflow you control | Claude API + tool use |
| Open-ended agent (you host compute) | Claude API agentic loop |
| Stateful agent with Anthropic-hosted workspace | Managed Agents (beta) |

## Current Model (Default)

I use **Claude Opus 4.8** (`claude-opus-4-8`) by default unless you specify otherwise. With adaptive thinking enabled, it handles complex reasoning automatically.

## Key Constraints

- I only add Anthropic SDK code to Anthropic projects. If I detect OpenAI imports or `gpt-*` references, I'll ask whether to switch to Claude first.
- I never guess SDK APIs—all code comes from official documentation.
- I keep your content stable (system prompts, tool definitions) before volatile content (timestamps, user input) for effective prompt caching.

## How to Invoke Me

- **General requests:** "Help me add tool use to my Python agent" or "How do I migrate to Opus 4.8?"
- **Subcommands:** `/claude-api managed-agents-onboard` (walks you through setting up a Managed Agent)

For any questions about the latest APIs, I'll fetch current documentation.

content.content:
# Skill Creator Guide Summary

## Core Purpose
The Skill Creator helps develop Claude skills through iterative testing and refinement. As the guide states: *"figure out where the user is in this process and then jump in and help them progress"* through drafting, testing, and improving skills.

## Main Workflow Steps

**1. Capture Intent**
- Understand what the skill should enable Claude to do
- Determine triggering contexts
- Define expected output formats
- Decide if test cases are needed

**2. Interview & Research**
Ask about edge cases, input/output formats, success criteria, and dependencies before writing anything.

**3. Write SKILL.md**
The guide emphasizes making descriptions somewhat assertive: *"make the skill descriptions a little bit 'pushy'"* to combat underutilization of skills by Claude.

**4. Test & Evaluate**
Run test cases with and without the skill in parallel. The guide stresses: *"GENERATE THE EVAL VIEWER *BEFORE* evaluating inputs yourself"* to get human feedback on outputs before making revisions.

**5. Iterate**
Improve based on user feedback, focusing on generalization rather than overfitting to test cases.

## Key Principles

- **Explain the why**: *"Try hard to explain the why behind everything you're asking"* rather than using rigid all-caps directives
- **Keep it lean**: Remove elements that aren't productive
- **Generalize**: Skills must work across many prompts, not just test examples
- **Look for patterns**: If all test runs independently create similar helper scripts, bundle them in the skill

## Communication Style
Adjust technical language based on user familiarity—briefly explain terms like "JSON" or "assertion" if needed rather than assuming knowledge.

content.text:
Perfect! I have successfully fetched all 17 skill SKILL.md files from the anthropics/skills repository. Here is the complete content for each:

## 1. Algorithmic Art
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/algorithmic-art/SKILL.md

This skill provides a framework for creating generative art using p5.js structured around three phases: Algorithmic Philosophy (creating a 4-6 paragraph manifesto), Conceptual Deduction (extracting thematic seeds), and p5.js Implementation. Key technical requirements include seeded randomness with `randomSeed()` and `noiseSeed()`, parameter design focusing on system properties, single self-contained HTML artifact structure using p5.js CDN, and a template foundation from `templates/viewer.html`. Critical principles emphasize algorithmic beauty over static composition with unique outputs from identical code, proper performance optimization, and visual hierarchy within randomness.

## 2. Canvas Design
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/canvas-design/SKILL.md

This skill guides visual art creation through two steps: establishing a design philosophy (aesthetic manifesto in 4-6 paragraphs) and expressing it visually on canvas as .pdf or .png files. The methodology emphasizes visual-first communication where ideas communicate through space, form, color, and composition rather than paragraphs. Key process steps include philosophy creation covering form, space, color, rhythm, and composition; conceptual threading of subtle thematic DNA; and canvas execution with museum-quality work. Execution standards demand flawless composition with proper margins, typography as design element from curated fonts, patterns building meaning through sustained viewing, and work appearing as though created by someone at the absolute top of their field.

## 3. Frontend Design
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/frontend-design/SKILL.md

This skill enables creation of distinctive, production-grade web interfaces prioritizing authentic design over generic aesthetics. Before coding, establish clear aesthetic direction considering the interface's purpose, target audience, tonal approach (brutalist to maximalist), technical constraints, and accessibility. Implementation priorities include distinctive typography choices that elevate, cohesive color systems using CSS variables with intentional accents, strategic animation at high-impact moments, unexpected spatial compositions with asymmetry and negative space, and atmospheric details like gradients and textures. Warnings explicitly advise against overused choices: generic fonts like Inter and Roboto, clichéd color schemes (especially purple gradients), and predictable layouts lacking context-specific character.

## 4. Theme Factory
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/theme-factory/SKILL.md

The Theme Factory is a styling toolkit for applying professional, cohesive visual themes to artifacts like presentations, documents, and web pages. It offers 10 pre-designed themes with curated color palettes and complementary font pairings: Ocean Depths, Sunset Boulevard, Forest Canopy, Modern Minimalist, Golden Hour, Arctic Frost, Desert Rose, Tech Innovation, Botanical Garden, and Midnight Galaxy. The primary workflow involves displaying the theme showcase, requesting user preference, obtaining explicit confirmation, and applying chosen colors and fonts throughout. Users can create custom themes by providing descriptions, with the system generating new theme options in established format, presenting for approval, then applying consistently across work. All themes ensure proper contrast and readability.

## 5. Slack GIF Creator
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/slack-gif-creator/SKILL.md

This Python toolkit creates Slack-optimized animated GIFs with specific dimensions: Emoji GIFs at 128x128 (recommended) and Message GIFs at 480x480, with frame rates between 10-30 FPS and 48-128 colors for optimal results. Core components include GIFBuilder for assembling and optimizing frames, Validators for confirming Slack requirements, Easing Functions for smooth motion transitions, and Frame Helpers for common drawing tasks. Animation approaches cover shake, pulse, bounce, rotation, fade, sliding, zooming, and particle burst effects with PIL library implementation guidance. The toolkit emphasizes creative flexibility using PIL primitives rather than rigid templates. File optimization strategies include reducing frame counts, limiting colors to 48, using smaller dimensions, removing duplicate frames, and enabling emoji-specific optimization modes.

## 6. DOCX
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/docx/SKILL.md

This skill handles creation, reading, editing, and manipulation of Word documents (.docx files) with professional formatting. It uses the `docx-js` JavaScript library via `npm install -g docx`. Core capabilities include supporting tables, images, hyperlinks, footnotes, headers/footers, and multi-column layouts. Critical technical rules specify page dimensions (US Letter = 12,240 × 15,840 DXA, where 1,440 DXA = 1 inch), table requirements (both `columnWidths` array AND individual cell widths in DXA units that must sum correctly), and explicit restrictions on Unicode bullets, newline characters, percentage-based table widths, and `ShadingType.SOLID`. Images require type parameter (png/jpg/etc) and alternative text properties. Smart quotes use XML entities (`“`, `’`, etc.). For editing: unpack XML, edit, repack with validation. XML editing guidelines specify "Claude" as default author for tracked changes using `<w:ins>` and `<w:del>` elements.

## 7. PDF
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/pdf/SKILL.md

This comprehensive guide covers PDF operations using Python libraries and command-line tools for reading, extracting, merging, splitting, and creating PDFs. Key Python libraries include pypdf (basic operations like merging, splitting, extracting metadata, rotating pages), pdfplumber (text and table extraction with layout preservation and DataFrame conversion), and reportlab (PDF creation from scratch with multiple pages and styled text). The guide warns never to use Unicode subscript/superscript characters as they render as black boxes; use XML markup tags instead. Command-line tools referenced include pdftotext (text extraction with layout preservation), qpdf (page manipulation and merging), and pdftk (legacy merging and rotating). Specialized tasks covered include OCR on scanned documents (requiring pytesseract), watermarking, image extraction, and password protection. The license is proprietary, with complete terms in LICENSE.txt.

## 8. PPTX
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/pptx/SKILL.md

This documentation describes presentation creation and editing toolkit for any `.pptx` file interaction. Reading presentations uses `python -m markitdown presentation.pptx` for text extraction, generate thumbnails, or unpack raw XML. Creation approaches include from template (unpack existing slides, modify, repack) or from scratch (use PptxGenJS when no reference exists). Design principles avoid plain text slides; each slide needs visual elements like images, charts, or icons. Key recommendations include topic-specific color palettes with one dominant color (60-70% weight), contrasting dark/light backgrounds strategically, one distinctive visual motif repeated throughout, varied layouts preventing monotony, consistent 0.3-0.5" spacing and 0.5" margins, 36-44pt titles and 14-16pt body text. Quality assurance workflow assumes initial renders contain issues: check for missing content, placeholder text, overlapping elements, text overflow, contrast problems. Use subagents for visual inspection by converting slides to JPEG, then systematically fix and re-verify before completion.

## 9. XLSX
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/xlsx/SKILL.md

This document outlines comprehensive guidelines for Excel file creation, editing, and analysis. Professional presentation uses consistent fonts (Arial, Times New Roman) throughout. Critical requirement: "Every Excel model MUST be delivered with ZERO formula errors (#REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #N/A, #NAME?)". Financial color coding (unless specified): blue text for hardcoded inputs and user-changeable values, black for formulas and calculations, green for cross-sheet links, red for external file references, yellow background for critical assumptions. Number formatting rules specify years as text strings ("2024"), currency with units in headers ("Revenue ($mm)"), zeros as dashes, percentages at 0.0% format, negative numbers in parentheses. Critical implementation: use formulas, not hardcoded values; Excel formulas must perform all calculations. Hardcoded values require source citations (document, date, reference, URLs). After creating/modifying files with formulas, run `scripts/recalc.py` to calculate values and detect errors. Library selection: pandas for data analysis, openpyxl for formulas and formatting.

## 10. Claude API
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/claude-api/SKILL.md

This skill guides building LLM-powered applications using Claude across Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, and cURL. It helps build and debug Claude API applications, optimize prompt caching to reduce costs and latency, migrate code between Claude model versions, implement Claude features (thinking, tool use, structured outputs, batches, file handling), and design agents using Claude API or Managed Agents (Anthropic's server-managed agent surface). The skill uses Claude Opus 4.8 (`claude-opus-4-8`) by default unless otherwise specified, with adaptive thinking enabled. Key constraint: only add Anthropic SDK code to Anthropic projects; if OpenAI imports or `gpt-*` references detected, ask whether to switch to Claude first. Never guess SDK APIs; all code comes from official documentation. Keep content stable (system prompts, tool definitions) before volatile content (timestamps, user input) for effective prompt caching.

## 11. MCP Builder
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/mcp-builder/SKILL.md

This comprehensive guide provides a structured four-phase approach for building high-quality MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers: (1) Deep Research and Planning - understanding MCP design patterns, protocol specifications, framework documentation; (2) Implementation - setting up project structure, core infrastructure, tool development; (3) Review and Testing - code quality verification and functional testing; (4) Evaluation Creation - building comprehensive test suites. Key design principles recommend balancing comprehensive API endpoint coverage with specialized workflow tools. Tools require clear naming conventions (like `github_create_issue`), actionable error messages with specific next steps, structured output schemas using `structuredContent`. Technical stack recommends TypeScript for high-quality SDK support and compatibility, alongside Streamable HTTP for remote servers and stdio for local implementations. Testing uses MCP Inspector tool (`npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector`). Evaluation requires creating 10 independent, complex, read-only test questions with verifiable answers in XML format.

## 12. Web Artifacts Builder
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/web-artifacts-builder/SKILL.md

This toolkit creates sophisticated Claude.ai HTML artifacts using React 18 + TypeScript + Vite + Parcel (bundling) + Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui. The three-phase workflow: (1) Initialize new React project via initialization script; (2) Develop artifact by modifying generated files; (3) Bundle everything into single, self-contained HTML file. Initial setup generates fully configured project with React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 3.4.1, pre-installed shadcn/ui components, Parcel bundling configuration. Bundling script processes project into `bundle.html`, "a self-contained artifact with all JavaScript, CSS, and dependencies inlined." Design principles emphasize avoiding common aesthetic pitfalls: excessive centered layouts, purple gradients, uniform rounded corners, Inter font usage. Additional resources include shadcn/ui documentation at ui.shadcn.com/docs/components. Testing is optional and recommended only after presenting the artifact if issues arise.

## 13. Web Application Testing
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/webapp-testing/SKILL.md

This resource provides guidance for testing local web applications using Playwright with Python. Available tools include `scripts/with_server.py` (manages server lifecycle for single or multiple servers) and example scripts in `examples/` directory. Recommended workflow uses decision tree approach: (1) For static HTML, read file directly to find selectors, write Playwright scripts; (2) For dynamic applications, determine if server is running; (3) If not running, use helper script to start it; (4) If running, follow "reconnaissance-then-action" pattern: navigate with waits, capture screenshots, identify selectors, execute actions. Critical best practice: "Wait for `page.wait_for_load_state('networkidle')` before inspection" on dynamic applications to ensure JavaScript execution completes before DOM inspection. Approach for scripts emphasizes running `python scripts/with_server.py --help` first to understand usage as black-box tool rather than reading source code directly. Toolkit supports verification of frontend functionality, UI debugging, screenshot capture, and browser logging.

## 14. Brand Guidelines
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/brand-guidelines/SKILL.md

This file contains Anthropic's official brand styling specifications. Brand color palette specifies six core colors: Primary Dark `#141413`, Primary Light `#faf9f5`, Mid Gray `#b0aea5`, Light Gray `#e8e6dc`, Orange Accent `#d97757`, Blue Accent `#6a9bcc`, Green Accent `#788c5d`. Typography standards: headings use Poppins font (Arial backup), body content uses Lora font (Georgia backup), with automatic system font fallback mechanisms. Key features apply styling intelligently through font selection based on text size (24pt+ receives heading treatment), automatic system font fallbacks, accent color cycling for non-text elements, and RGB color precision for brand consistency. Technical foundation leverages python-pptx's RGBColor class, requires no font installation, operates seamlessly with pre-installed system fonts.

## 15. Doc Co-Authoring
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/doc-coauthoring/SKILL.md

This workflow guides collaborative document creation through three structured stages: (1) Context Gathering - user provides all relevant context while Claude asks clarifying questions; (2) Refinement & Structure - documents built section-by-section using repeatable cycle: clarifying questions → brainstorming options → curation → drafting → iterative refinement; (3) Reader Testing - completed document validated by testing with fresh Claude instances to catch blind spots. Offer this workflow when users mention documentation tasks like writing specs, proposals, decision docs, or similar structured content. Notable features use artifacts or files to scaffold document structure with placeholders, employ `str_replace` for surgical edits rather than full reprints, recommend starting with complex sections first, include final user review step before completion, suggest linking conversation as documentation of development process. The workflow prioritizes ensuring documents actually work for readers lacking authors' context.

## 16. Internal Communications
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/internal-comms/SKILL.md

This skill helps craft internal communications using company's preferred formats. Communication types supported include 3P updates (Progress, Plans, Problems), company newsletters, FAQ responses, status reports, leadership updates, project updates, incident reports. How it works: identify communication type, then system loads relevant guideline file from examples directory. Each guideline provides specific formatting, tone, and content requirements tailored to that communication style. Available guidelines include four main template files: one for 3P-style team updates, one for organization-wide newsletters, one for FAQ answers, one for general communications not fitting other categories. If communication needs don't align with existing guidelines, users can request clarification or additional context about preferred format.

## 17. Skill Creator
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/skill-creator/SKILL.md

The Skill Creator helps develop Claude skills through iterative testing and refinement by figuring out where the user is and helping them progress through drafting, testing, and improving skills. Main workflow steps: (1) Capture Intent - understand what skill should enable Claude to do, determine triggering contexts, define expected output formats, decide if test cases needed; (2) Interview & Research - ask about edge cases, input/output formats, success criteria, dependencies before writing; (3) Write SKILL.md - make descriptions somewhat assertive ("pushy") to combat underutilization; (4) Test & Evaluate - run test cases with/without skill in parallel, GENERATE EVAL VIEWER BEFORE evaluating inputs yourself for human feedback; (5) Iterate - improve based on feedback, focusing on generalization not overfitting. Key principles: explain the why rather than rigid all-caps directives, keep it lean removing unproductive elements, generalize so skills work across many prompts not just test examples, look for patterns bundling similar helper scripts. Adjust technical language based on user familiarity explaining terms like "JSON" or "assertion" rather than assuming knowledge.

---

All 17 skills have been successfully fetched with complete content. Each skill file is available at the pattern: `https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anthropics/skills/main/skills/{skill-name}/SKILL.md`
